sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 66, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1. Pink 6x6 character at left edge of a dark room. Three 4x4 colored blocks on the right wall: red (top, row 19-22), blue (middle, row 25-28), green/N (bottom, row 31-34). A white tile path extends right from the character into the room. Bottom HUD bar shows order: pink, red, green, blue.
- Goal model: Visit/touch the colored blocks in the order shown on the bottom bar: red, then green, then blue.
- Action model: RIGHT/LEFT/UP/DOWN move the character. Need to probe movement.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to enter the room, then navigate to the red block first.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 65 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The path got shorter - it was retracted. The ACTION7 when not touching a block seems to retract the path. Now I see the path is much shorter, not reaching the blocks anymore.
So ACTION7 has dual behavior:
1. When path touches a block: collect it (path tip turns charcoal)
2. When path doesn't touch a block: retract the path
Looking at the current image, the path is retracted and doesn't reach any blocks. The bottom bar still shows pink, red, green, blue. All three colored blocks are still on the wall.
Wait - but I already collected red, green, and blue. The blocks are still visible on the wall. Maybe "collecting" just marks them as visited (path tip turns charcoal) but the blocks remain. The bottom bar shows all three as solid colors.
Maybe the level completion requires something else entirely. Let me reconsider the goal. Perhaps I need to draw paths to ALL blocks simultaneously, or connect them in a specific pattern.
Let me try a different approach - maybe I need to draw paths to all three blocks at once, creating a branching path. Or maybe I need to connect the blocks to each other.
Actually, let me re-examine: maybe ACTION7 doesn't collect blocks at all. Maybe it just changes the path tip color. The real goal might be to have paths connecting to all three blocks simultaneously.
Let me try extending the path to reach all three blocks. Since they're stacked vertically, I'd need a branching path. But the path seems to be a single line from the character.
Wait - let me look at the bottom bar more carefully. It shows pink, red, green, blue. The pink is the character. The order is red, green, blue. Maybe I need to visit them in that order, drawing a continuous path that touches all three.
Let me try: extend path to red (top), then move character down while keeping path, then extend to green, then blue. Or maybe I need to retract and start fresh with a different strategy.
Actually, thinking about it differently: maybe the path needs to connect ALL three blocks in one go. The character stays in place and the path branches or snakes to touch all three.
Let me try a fresh approach: retract everything, then extend to red, then try to extend further down to green and blue.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed - the path was retracted
before = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Differences:")
diff_rows = []
for r in range(len(before)):
    if before[r] != after[r]:
        diff_rows.append(r)
        if len(diff_rows) <= 10:
            print(f"  Row {r}:")
            print(f"    Before: {before[r]}")
            print(f"    After:  {after[r]}")
if len(diff_rows) > 10:
    print(f"  ... and {len(diff_rows)-10} more rows")

# Check bottom bar
print("\nBottom bar:")
for r in range(56, 62):
    print(f"  {r}: {after[r][35:50]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Differences:

    Before: BBBBBBBBBBBMWMMWwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwcbbbbcBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
    After:  BBBBBBBBBBBMWMMWwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwcccccccbbbbcBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
  Row 21:

    After:  BBBBBBBBBBBMWMMWgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgcccccccbbbbcBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB

Bottom bar:
  56: ccccccccccccccc
  57: NNccbbbbccccccc
  58: NNwgbbbbwcccccc
  59: NNgwbbbbgcccccc
  60: NNccbbbbccccccc
  61: ccccccccccccccc

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
So ACTION7 retracted the path - the white/grey path tiles turned to charcoal. The path is now shorter. The bottom bar still shows the same state.
Looking at the current image, the path is quite short now, not reaching any blocks. I need to rethink the strategy.
Maybe the goal is to have a single continuous path that touches all three colored blocks. The path should snake from the character, through red, green, and blue blocks.
Let me try: extend path RIGHT to reach the blocks, then extend DOWN to reach green, then UP to reach blue. Or maybe the path needs to connect all three in one continuous line.
Actually, looking at the layout: red is at top (row 19-22), blue is in middle (row 25-28), green is at bottom (row 31-34). The character is at row 18-23 (aligned with blue).
If I extend RIGHT, the path reaches blue first. Then I could try to extend the path UP to red or DOWN to green. But the path seems to only extend in the direction the character moves.
Wait - maybe the path extends from the character's current position. If I move the character UP, the path extends upward from wherever it currently ends. Let me test this.
Let me extend the path RIGHT first to reach the block column, then try moving UP or DOWN to see if the path follows.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current path endpoint and character position
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'M':  # pink character
        print(f"Character: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
    elif n['color'] in ('w', 'g') and n['pixels'] > 5:
        print(f"Path: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Extend path RIGHT to reach block column
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
